Hey, it's Sean and Andre. Today we are talking about the soap opera series. You can see in front of you here, this is a 10,000 foot view on the left. You'll see that they're A through E. These are different types of soap opera series, meaning the different lengths. And Andre will walk through and explain how there are so many different ways that we can use the soap opera series. The way to think about it is that the soap opera series really is like the intellectual slash philosophical core of the art of email. The name says it all. What we're doing is we're pulling attention and using principles of serialized narratives. So what we are opening loops and closing loops within emails to pull attention through them emails. We're opening and closing loops across emails. We use cliff hangers. We use many of the devices of serialized narrative to continue to pull an audience's attention forward in a way that feels like you're watching a really engaging movie or a really interesting serialized television show. Or you're reading a good book and you get to the end of a chapter, for example, and there's a cliff hanger and you wonder what happens next. But it's not answered in the next chapter. It's not answered until five or six chapters later. So you're constantly engaged as the reader or the person experiencing it because of the way it's set up to pull our attention forward. I mentioned in a previous in the relationship building series that the soap opera series is like the Swiss army knife of the art of email. So there's so many ways that you can use it. And I'm going to hand it over to Andre now to just talk through just at a high level when he has this, you know, A, B, C, D, and E, like all the different ways to think about the soap opera series and what it means that it's this Swiss army knife that you can do almost anything with a soap opera series. So Andre, take it away. Yeah, I like that idea of a Swiss army knife because that's exactly what it is. So these different colors, yeah, A through D, E, they could represent themes. They could represent different products if you sell multiple products. It's anything and it can be any length. So the one at the bottom here is not so long. And the rest are a bit shorter. You won't typically have them as short as I've written them here, but you know, I could fit some text to the right. But soap opera series are, you know, a week long, two weeks, three weeks, whatever. But it takes people along a journey, as Sean said. So there is an entry point and there's an end point. And part of what a soap opera series does is it deepens your world. So it's a part of world building. World building exists on your website everywhere. If you're putting content on there and it's becoming feelers into your world and leading to your opt-in pages or page and then email is a great way to do world building because, you know, we create these journeys that people then go along. So one way to think of this, I guess, is you could use this as a container to do a mini course, to talk about a certain thing or theme. But the last thing is they don't necessarily sell or they don't have to sell it all. They can sell, they can be subtle about selling or setting up a sale further along. So you could have a story-powered promotion that hangs off one of these. So for example, let's say you write the series and you call it season one of whatever this thing is. And as it gets nearer the end, it sets up this desire for somebody to get your thing to solve a problem. So at the end, there could be a bridge email that then moves people to a story-powered promotion, for example. But, you know, you could have as many of these things as you want scattered all over your email system and none of them sell. All they do is they just deepen the world and it really builds trust and attention. So I think that's one way to think of it. It is quite general like that. I mean, you can use these things for just so many different things. Yeah. Yeah, I'll throw out a few examples of how to think about this from a brainstorming perspective. Because it doesn't have to be complicated. And that's one of the dangers of, you know, we see something like this, we think, oh, wow, that one at the bottom looks terrifying. And I like the 10 or 12 or whatever. And how would I ever create that? It's not that complicated. And you could think of something like, perhaps you read a really good book. And that book, you know, like the book, it could be fiction, nonfiction, doesn't matter. The book is kind of the hook that you or you listen to a podcast and it gave you an idea. And then and that idea you think is important. So you spend five emails, seven emails, 10 emails, but you're, you're, you're, you can, you can describe the experience of, you know, there's, I read in the, you know, this is I'm not, not writing copy here at all. But you can have the hook could be something around that you read this book and it, there was an idea or you heard this podcast and there was this idea that you thought, wow, that's, that's really fascinating. And you couldn't let it go. You really wanted to learn more about it. So you, you explored, then, you know, the first thing that you found was this, you know, that could be an email. And then you realize, well, you know, that was just the tip of the iceberg. That's a great open loop, right? There's an implication there that just more and you could, you could then, you could create this journey that mirrors the journey that you experienced. Now, maybe you experienced the journey over a course of five hours or five days, who knows, but you can recreate a journey. If it was meaningful for you, if you thought, wow, when I, let's just say for the sake of argument, it was a really good book. And you, you read the book and you, and we've all had this experience where we like, wow, that was like, that experience was incredible. And you want to go back and maybe that book had a central idea, you think to yourself, that's, that's really something I need my audience to understand, like that central idea. Well, now you've got the book, you've got the idea, you have your experience of it. So now you're starting to see the ingredients to begin to put them together. And the soap opera series has this, has a beginning, but the thing that it has that's so powerful is it has an end. And it, because there's a beginning and an end, there's a natural tension between those two points. There's a bringing to a close of, of energy and attention. And there's a, if you can see the way that the thematic arc is written, is drawn here, that arc, you can see the tension rising, right? So you may have picked up a book, I'm just making this up, but maybe you decided to read a book that you had avoided for a long time, because you didn't like, you heard the author on a podcast or something, you didn't like what the author had to say. And so you set that up. But in, in, in, you have some implication there that, you know, I thought, I thought this, I thought I didn't like this person, right? I thought this person's, you know, viewpoint was nonsense, whatever, there's implication there that you thought it was, but maybe not. So the tension starts to rise. Well, now as the reader, I'm interested, I'm like, okay, well, what happened? You know, you, you had a view of this idea or this author or this interview or whatever, you had this idea. And then when you explored it, you began to question your position, like, well, now I'm, I'm having that experience with you. And that's the kind of journey you can take somebody on. It doesn't have to be, we can, we can all make this far more complex than it needs to be. It could, it can be something as simple, I had this experience a few months ago, it's something as simple as hearing something said in a podcast. And it was just, it was one statement that was said in that statement. It stuck with me. And then it became a multiple chat threads and it became, it took on a life of its own, because I was so impacted by that one idea. Well, that, that couldn't have turned easily into a soap opera series. It's just for, if you're, when you're listening to this, this overview, watching this overview, it's important not to get caught up in that, wow, this looks really complicated. Make it simple at first, just choose something that is interesting to you. Because if it's interesting to you, it's likely to be interesting to your audience. Conversely, if it's not interesting to you, it's going to be really hard to make it interesting to somebody else. But just something that captures your attention or something that has captured your attention, that you thought, wow, that's, that's fascinating, right? I really enjoyed that. And it could be an idea, it could be a position you used to, something you used to believe, and then you had an experience and realized that that wasn't true. Like just, just kind of sketch that out and see what that looks like and see how the tension can rise over time and then resolve at an end point that either completely resolves or sets up more attention for what's next, because you can certainly put a soap opera series after another soap opera series. Or as Andre mentioned, you could have a soap opera series that builds tension for an idea that, that leads someone to a fork in the road. And one, one way is doing nothing. And the other way is maybe looking at a, to continue down the road for a story powered promotion, for example. So there's, there's so many ways to use this. And one last thing to say before I hand it back over to you, Andre, you said something earlier that's really important to point out, because it's such a powerful idea, which is you can think about the soap opera series as season one. And one of the reasons that's so powerful is implicit in season one is that there's a season two. And so the moment you say this, and here's season one of X, and you don't have to necessarily use that language, but you can use similar language to suggest that this is not the only experience they're going to have like this. And the moment you, you, you suggest season one, you're suggesting there's even more, which pulls attention forward as well. So there's so many ways to use the soap opera series, tired of hearing my own voice describing it, but it's, it's so exciting. Is it such, it's such an important part of, I had to think about the art of email. So back over to you, Andre, so I can do your voice, not mine. So in, in module two, we, we link out to, to a podcast series. It's season one, and it's called soft voice. And I linked, I linked to it in purpose because a few months ago, I was looking for podcasts to listen to. And I found this thing, it, it sounded intriguing, you know, soft voice. It, there was, there was a nice picture. And, and I started, and downloaded it episode one, and I started to, to listen to it. And then I, it was just so engrossing. It's like sucked me in because, I mean, well, this podcast was done really well, really good narration. But, but the storytelling behind it, that, that was driving me, this thing forward was, was amazing. And then I downloaded episode two and episode three. But what was really like, it lit me up when, when I figured out what they were doing is maybe twice. So there's two or three times during each episode in each episode, like, I don't know, half an hour, for example, it, it goes to like an ad break. And the ad break is for a mental health service. And so it's like a SaaS service where you can, if you have a mental health issue, you could contact the service and they will connect you with the professional and then you can jump on zoom, whatever, and, and discuss stuff. Right. And, but so the underlying thesis of this podcast is this, is this character and this thing that she's, she's got these voices in her head and this, this voice, of course, soft voice. I just thought it was such a nice example of what a soap opera sequence series is, you know, what it can be. It's this thing that's, it's a journey that's, that's entertaining and it's educational. And within the context of that, you can recommend stuff. But at the same time, people get drawn to whatever this thing is. So, you know, if you have a service where you help people about mental health, then having that podcast as, as your soap opera series could be an amazing way to, to demonstrate what we're trying to do here, which is, which is both trust and attention. So for example, for us, if we bring this back to us now, we could have a series one could be awareness series two could be engaged and series three could be conversion. And we can write this as a, each one is maybe 10 emails long. And then within the context of, of our email system, people could self, well, can self select at any point. So maybe in the PS of certain value newsletters, there could be a PS we've, we've just wrapped up series one of, of our awareness, whatever. And if you've interested in it, it's a 10 part series. And we think you'll enjoy it. And that's it. It just sits there in the PS of a, of a certain email or newsletter, or can be part of, of a relational building series. And that then triggers this thing, which then deepens that experience and that, and that relationship that we try to establish with somebody. When it gets to the end, there doesn't have to be an offer, although there can be. Yeah. So I think we've, we're getting over and over here, but I think that's, you know, this is about being preeminent, whereas we, we're doing things that are pulling people forward in, in a way that's enriching for them. And I think it's important to draw some attention to the fact that you may not have, you may not need to know the end point to get started. I think we shared somewhere, I mean, you shared somewhere the, a quote from, I think it was JJ Abrams talking about loss, where they, there were so many questions in the beginning of lost on the pilot that they didn't have answers to yet, but they just, they created the pilot and feel like, well, we'll answer all these later. And you, you could do that. You certainly could have, you know, it's, let me cautious to, you know, don't just jump in and start writing and then hope everybody follows along because really a good soap opera series. And this is true of really all good marketing is really all about what's next. The soap opera series is that that's, that's the emphasis. It's all in, we say what's next, it's not meaning the next big thing that you're doing, but it's, it's the next thought. It's the next open loop. It's the next reveal. It's the next insight. It's the next episode. It's always what's next. That's what draws our attention forward. So you don't have to know an end point to set up some things that are next, you know, a thought or, or sort of an overall idea. And I'll put this into a context. This is not a soap opera series we're thinking about, but it's, it's something that if, if I were to, if I were listening to this, switching roles, and if I were listening to this as a customer and thinking to myself, okay, well, what kinds of things could I think about creating a soap opera series around? You know, I had an experience in the last few weeks or so where I listened to Tim Ferris interview Jimmy Wales, Jimmy Wales is the founder of Wikipedia. And it was, it was the interview was interesting. But one of the things that, that Jimmy Wales mentioned was how important Ein Rand's book, The Fountainhead was to him. But he, he suggested that it was what he, what he thought was so important about it was not what generally people say is important about it. And this was the amount of fifth, sixth, 10th, 100th time I've heard someone reference Ein Rand. Ein Rand is very sort of a controversial figure. I looked at some of her books before and I just wasn't drawn to them. But finally, for whatever reason, I think it was probably because Jimmy Wales, he gave it a certain context. He framed it. And, and because of how he framed it, and I didn't actually look to see it, suggested read time is 19 hours and 54 minutes or something. I didn't notice that part because it was on Kindle. But I finally just said, okay, I'm going to read The Fountainhead. And I could do an entire series on that experience. But if you, if you think about it, and I wouldn't need to do it linear like that, I could actually start with, I mean, I could start with things like, you know, I, I wait, you know, I avoided reading Ein Rand for 15 years and finally decided to read it. Okay, well, that's, that's curious. Like, why, why would someone wait, avoid something for 15 years and then do it? So now we've got a little bit, that's a kind of curiosity that just naturally shows up. Or, and there are a million different ways to do this. But this is just something that just kind of happened while I, you know, I was doing everything else in my life. It was, it's just an idea that I could run with. And maybe the only reason I use that idea is to set up a larger idea of what happens when you believe that you avoid something or believe something for a really long time, and then you actually challenge that belief. Like that could be the underlying theme. And I could just be using my experience with the fountain head as an example. And that could then set other things in motion. And there's so many ways to use these ideas. And that's why the soap opera series is so incredibly powerful, is that it's just a natural way to get somebody's attention. And then to pull that attention forward and direct it in different ways in a way that's very natural to the person who's experiencing it. Like it's, it's, when you do it well, somebody wants to know what's next. Their attention to pull, they're waiting to find out what you have to say next. And think about what a resource that is, and what a competitive advantage it is, that when your emails show up in somebody's inbox, the person is waiting for them. They want to know what's next. That, in general, that's not how it, how email works. You know, we don't, our experience in general with most email marketing isn't, oh, good, I'm so glad that arrived. Yet that's, that's what you can create with that feeling with a soap opera series. All right, I'll do anything left to say for this one, we've talked longer than we probably imagined we would on this, but I think it's just because we both, we both love this so much. So I'll let you take it across the finish line. Right. I think it's just worth mentioning one, one last thing, which is the fact that E zero, so this, this entry point, these get triggered when, when somebody does something, when, when someone raises their hand, essentially raises their hand by clicking on a link. So you can set up as many of these things in your, in your email system as you want. You could have hundreds of these things and they're all assets because they take people on journeys, but none of these journeys are, are, are linear in the sense that they opt in and then they just go through these things. You know, they don't, many people, if you had a hundred of these things, you know, people aren't going to go through all a hundred of those things. Maybe they'll only go through three of those things, but depending on, on how people self-select and end up at this entry point here. So I think that's, that's the beauty of these things. You can, you can write these things if you have an idea for, for a little journey you want to send up somebody, somebody down, you just write this, you just write it up, stick it inside of your email service provider and create a trigger and then you get to insert that trigger wherever it makes sense. It could be in your signature files, it can be anywhere and people will then trigger these things all day long, every day while you're sleeping and it'll send people down these, these, these little rabbit holes that, that do these amazing things and, and yeah. So I think that's, that's what I'll end on. Perfect. Thanks everybody for your attention. We always appreciate it. Thank you.
